did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and computer vision techniques for dermatology tasks. Specifically, it proposes a hybrid architecture called ITSU-EfficientNet (combining UNet and EfficientNet) for multi-class segmentation of skin diseases, optimized using an Improved Tuna Swarm Optimization (ITSO) algorithm. Additionally, it employs a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture for classifying melanoma versus noncancerous injuries on the HAM10000 and ISIC-2018 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled 'RESEARCH' in the header. It presents original experimental work, including the design of a new hybrid architecture (ITSU-EfficientNet), the development of an optimization algorithm (ITSO), and the evaluation of a ViT-based model on specific datasets (HAM10000 and ISIC-2018) with reported performance metrics such as accuracy and FNR.

title: Multi‑class segmentation skin diseases using improved tuna swarm‑based U‑EfficientNet
journal: Journal of Engineering and Applied Science
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of early melanoma detection by proposing a hybrid deep learning framework for multi-class skin disease segmentation and classification. The authors designed the ITSU-EfficientNet architecture, which combines EfficientNet and UNet, and optimized its parameters using an Improved Tuna Swarm Optimization (ITSO) algorithm. Additionally, a Vision Transformer (ViT) model was developed to classify melanoma versus noncancerous injuries. The models were evaluated on the HAM10000 and ISIC-2018 datasets using metrics such as accuracy, false negative rate (FNR), and computation time. The proposed ViT model achieved an average accuracy of 99.78% with a 10.43% FNR on the ISIC-2018 dataset, and 99.16% accuracy with a 9.38% FNR on the HAM10000 dataset. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods in improving diagnostic precision and reducing computational errors compared to baseline classifiers.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied as a dangerous form of skin cancer; specifically classified in the HAM10000 dataset (abbreviated as 'mel') and as part of the binary classification task (melanoma vs. noncancerous injuries) on both HAM10000 and ISIC-2018 datasets.

all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a hybrid architecture (ITSU-EfficientNet) for multi-class segmentation of skin diseases. It reports segmentation accuracy (e.g., 99.78% for ITSU-EfficientNet) and error rates for lesion segmentation on HAM1000 and ISIC-2018 datasets.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories (e.g., melanoma vs. noncancerous, or 7-class skin disease labels)
all_task_types.details: The paper employs a Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture for classification tasks. It performs binary classification (melanoma vs. noncancerous injuries) and multi-class classification (7 classes including melanoma, nevus, BCC, etc.) on HAM1000 and ISIC-2018 datasets, reporting accuracy, sensitivity, and false negative rate (FNR).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of dermoscopic images of skin lesions, also referred to as HAM1000 in the text. It contains images of various skin classes including melanoma, melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, and vascular lesions.

use.training: Used for training the Vision Transformer (ViT) model and the ITSU-EfficientNet segmentation model.
use.validation: Used for validation/evaluation of the models.
use.testing: Used for testing the models, with reported accuracy of 99.16% and FNR of 9.38%.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2018
datasets_used.description: A dataset released by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) containing dermoscopic images. The text states it includes more than 12,500 pictures, with more than 10,000 pictures divided into seven course categories for classification tasks.

use.training: Used for training the Vision Transformer (ViT) model and the ITSU-EfficientNet segmentation model.
use.validation: Used for validation/evaluation of the models.
use.testing: Used for testing the models, with reported accuracy of 99.78% and FNR of 10.43%.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, false negative rate (FNR), specificity, precision, sensitivity, F1 score, and Matthew correlation coefficient (MCC), along with computation time.

segmentation.notes: Segmentation results were computed using the mean of segmentation precision for each image. ITSU-EfficientNet processed 100 images in 29.4356s.

classification.notes: The proposed ViT model achieved an average accuracy of 99.78% and 10.43% FNR with a computation time of 134.4632s.
The proposed ViT model achieved an average accuracy of 99.16% and 9.38% FNR with a computation time of 133.4782s.

architecture_used.main_model: Two architectures were used: 1) ITSU-EfficientNet (a hybrid segmentation model combining EfficientNet and UNet) optimized by Improved Tuna Swarm Optimization (ITSO); 2) Vision Transformer (ViT) for classification.
architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported for the specific models in the provided text, though ViT is noted to perform well on benchmarks like ImageNet.
architecture_used.input_size: ViT can take input images of any size without scaling or cropping. Specific input dimensions for ITSU-EfficientNet are not reported.

ITSU-EfficientNet.components: Combines EfficientNet and UNet. Specifically, EfficientNet B7 replaces the encoder portion of the UNet. The decoder consists of 3x3 convolutional blocks that do not sample feature maps after each block to concatenate and transmit encoder features. A 1x1 transformation is used after the last block to create the segmentation map.
ITSU-EfficientNet.optimization: Improved Tuna Swarm Optimization (ITSO) is used to fine-tune the adjustable parameters of the hybrid model to minimize information loss.

ViT.components: Uses a transformer model with multiple image patches instead of convolutional layers. Input images are reduced and converted into N patches. Each patch of dimensions (P, P) is flattened into (1, p^2) and transmitted via a single feed-forward layer with an embedding matrix to create linear patch projections. Learnable [class] embeddings are concatenated with patch embeddings. The sequence is passed to a transformer encoder composed of L identical layers of multi-head self-attention (MSA) and multilayer perceptron (MLP) blocks. The MLP block uses GeLU activation. The top components of the sequence from the last encoding layer are passed to an exogenous classifier.

training_details.optimization_algorithm: Improved Tuna Swarm Optimization (ITSO) for ITSU-EfficientNet parameters. ITSO combines Tuna Swarm Optimization (TSO) with Pelican optimization algorithm solution updating behavior.
training_details.data_augmentation: Proper data augmentation approaches were utilized for training the ViT architecture with both synthetic and raw data.

classification_or_output_head.ViT: Exogenous classifier receiving top components of the sequence from the last encoding layer. Used for binary classification (melanoma vs. noncancerous injuries) and multi-class classification (7 classes).

preprocessing_techniques: Median filtering to remove image artifacts and noise
Reflection and artifact removal using simple thresholding calculation (TR1 = 0.85, TR2 = 0.097) to identify and exclude regions of artifacts
Data augmentation (synthetic and raw data) for training the ViT architecture
Segmentation of the Region of Interest (ROI) from input color skin pictures
Rotation and translation changes applied to improve ROI pictures

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The ViT architecture was trained using both synthetic and raw data with proper data augmentation approaches to improve performance.
To address dataset imbalance, images and labels were replaced with a vector of images and weights.
Rotation and translation changes were applied to improve the Region of Interest (ROI) pictures.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid architecture ITSU-EfficientNet, which combines EfficientNet and UNet optimized by Improved Tuna Swarm Optimization (ITSO), effectively improves segmentation accuracy for multi-class skin disease detection.

The Vision Transformer (ViT) model demonstrated high diagnostic precision for classifying melanoma versus noncancerous injuries, achieving an average accuracy of 99.78% with a 10.43% False Negative Rate (FNR) on the ISIC-2018 dataset.
On the HAM10000 dataset, the proposed ViT model achieved an average accuracy of 99.16% with a 9.38% FNR, outperforming baseline classifiers such as MSVM, ELM, and Naive Bayes.

The ITSO algorithm successfully accelerates convergence and minimizes information loss during the learning stage by combining Tuna Swarm Optimization with Pelican optimization solution updating behavior.

The study confirms that deep learning-based CAD systems can reduce diagnostic errors and the burden on dermatologists by providing reliable, objective analysis of dermoscopic images compared to subjective visual assessment.
Data augmentation using both synthetic and raw data is effective in improving the performance of the ViT architecture in handling the lack of large, balanced medical image datasets.
important_remarks: The paper contains significant translation artifacts and terminology errors (e.g., 'generous keratosis' for benign keratosis, 'vascular injury' for vascular lesion, 'fish swarm' for tuna swarm), which may indicate poor quality control or reliance on automated translation tools.

The study relies exclusively on two public datasets (HAM10000 and ISIC-2018) without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting the generalizability of the findings to real-world clinical settings.

The reported accuracy for the proposed ViT model is exceptionally high (99.78% on ISIC-2018 and 99.16% on HAM10000), which is unusually strong for skin lesion classification and warrants scrutiny regarding potential data leakage or overly optimistic evaluation protocols.

The False Negative Rate (FNR) for melanoma detection is reported as 10.43% (ISIC-2018) and 9.38% (HAM10000); in a clinical context, a ~10% miss rate for melanoma is critically high and poses significant safety risks, contradicting the claim of 'high diagnostic precision'.
The paper does not report standard deviation or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, making it impossible to assess the statistical significance or stability of the results.
No formal hypothesis testing or p-values are provided to support the claim that the proposed model significantly outperforms baseline classifiers (MSVM, ELM, Naive Bayes).

The segmentation performance is evaluated using 'accuracy rate' and 'error rate' rather than standard segmentation metrics like Dice coefficient or IoU, which are more appropriate for evaluating boundary precision in medical image segmentation.

The computational time reported (e.g., 134.4632s for ViT on ISIC-2018) is not normalized per image or batch in a way that allows direct comparison with other deep learning models, and the efficiency gains claimed are not rigorously benchmarked against state-of-the-art efficient architectures.

The dataset imbalance issue is acknowledged but addressed only via weighted vectors and data augmentation; the specific class distribution and the impact of this imbalance on the minority classes (e.g., melanoma) are not detailed in the results.

The 'Improved Tuna Swarm Optimization' (ITSO) is a novel meta-heuristic algorithm introduced in this work; its effectiveness is claimed but lacks comparison against other standard optimization techniques or ablation studies isolating the contribution of ITSO versus the architecture itself.
